rows.v: Nigiri, Sashimi, Temaki, Gunkanmaki, Chirashi, Inari
The foundational presentations — everything else on the menu builds from these forms.
The word 'sushi' doesn't mean raw fish — it refers to the vinegared rice; the raw-fish part is optional, which is why plenty of sushi is cooked or vegetarian.
Hosomaki, Futomaki, Uramaki, California Roll, Spicy Tuna Roll, Dragon Roll
Rice-outside vs. rice-inside rolls range from traditional to Western-style creations.

The inside-out roll was invented in the United States, not Japan — chefs flipped the rice to the outside to hide the dark seaweed that put off Western diners, and the style only later spread back to Japan.
Oshizushi, Temari Sushi, Chakinzushi, Aburi Nigiri, Narezushi
Less common shapes that showcase regional techniques beyond hand-formed nigiri.
Sushi was originally a preservation method, not a fresh food — fish was packed in fermented rice for months, and the sour rice was thrown away; only the pickled fish was eaten.
Maguro (Tuna), Otoro (Fatty Tuna), Sake (Salmon), Hamachi (Yellowtail), Saba (Mackerel), Tai (Sea Bream)
The fish toppings that define a sushi counter's quality — ask what's freshest that day.
At a real omakase, much of the nigiri is pre-seasoned with a soy glaze or wasabi, so dipping it in soy sauce isn't refinement — it overrides the balance the chef built into each piece.
Ebi (Shrimp), Unagi (Eel), Tako (Octopus), Uni (Sea Urchin), Ikura (Salmon Roe), Tamago (Egg)
Beyond fish — these toppings round out a balanced omakase progression.
The 'battleship' roll (gunkan-maki) was invented in 1941 specifically so chefs could serve soft, loose toppings like sea urchin and salmon roe that would slide right off a normal piece of nigiri.
Shari (Sushi Rice), Nori (Seaweed), Wasabi, Gari (Pickled Ginger), Shoyu (Soy Sauce), Nikiri (Glaze)
The supporting cast — rice seasoning, seaweed, and condiments that shape every bite.
Most of the bright green 'wasabi' served outside Japan isn't wasabi at all — it's dyed horseradish and mustard, because real wasabi is expensive, perishable, and must be grated fresh.
Miso Soup, Edamame, Agari (Green Tea), Sake, Japanese Beer, Wine & Sushi
Round out the meal — pair lighter dishes with sake or beer, richer ones with wine.
Sake is often NOT paired with sushi in Japan — since both are rice-based, many purists prefer green tea or a crisp wine, saving sake for dishes that aren't already built on rice.
Omakase, Eating Order, Soy & Wasabi Etiquette, Chopstick Rules, Itadakimasu & Gochisousama
Knowing these basics turns an intimidating sushi counter into a comfortable one.
Two everyday chopstick habits are taboo at a Japanese table — standing them upright in a bowl of rice and passing food chopstick-to-chopstick — because both mirror rituals performed at funerals.
